event: Stone Age communities of hunter-gatherers inhabit the West African savanna and forest regions of present-day Guinea.
Neolithic peoples develop early agriculture, cultivating millet and yams across the region's varied landscapes.
Iron-working spreads through West Africa, enabling more advanced farming and settled village life in the area.
Bantu-related and Mande-speaking agricultural communities expand across the region's grasslands and forests.
Northeastern Guinea falls within the orbit of the Ghana Empire's trans-Saharan gold and salt trade networks.
Sundiata Keita founds the Mali Empire, incorporating much of upper Guinea, including the heartland near the Niger River source.
Mansa Musa of Mali undertakes his famous pilgrimage to Mecca, with Guinea's gold-rich highlands feeding the empire's wealth.
Portuguese navigators begin exploring the West African coast, reaching the Guinea shoreline and initiating European contact.
Portuguese explorers map and name stretches of the Guinea coast, opening it to maritime trade.
The Atlantic slave trade intensifies along the Guinea coast as European powers establish coastal trading posts.
The decline of the Mali Empire leaves a patchwork of smaller Mande and Susu states across the interior.
Fulani Muslims establish the theocratic Imamate of Futa Jallon in the central highlands after a series of jihads.
Futa Jallon becomes a major Islamic state and a center of learning, trade, and Quranic scholarship in West Africa.
France declares a protectorate over coastal areas, beginning formal French involvement in the region.
France signs a treaty with the rulers of Futa Jallon, extending French influence into the interior highlands.
The Berlin Conference formalizes the European Scramble for Africa, assigning Guinea to the French sphere of influence.
France establishes French Guinea as a distinct colonial territory separate from Senegal.
French forces capture the Mandinka leader Samori Ture, ending his Wassoulou Empire's resistance to colonization.
French Guinea is incorporated into the federation of French West Africa, administered from Dakar.
France abolishes the Imamate of Futa Jallon, completing colonial subjugation of the territory.
Guinea becomes a French overseas territory, and Africans gain limited political representation under the French Union.
Sekou Toure helps organize trade unions and the Guinean branch of the pan-African Rassemblement Democratique Africain.
Sekou Toure's Democratic Party of Guinea wins territorial elections, and he becomes head of the local government.
Guinea overwhelmingly rejects de Gaulle's French Community in a referendum and declares independence on 2 October, the first French colony in sub-Saharan Africa to do so.
France abruptly withdraws all aid and administrators in retaliation, and Guinea joins the United Nations in December.
Guinea introduces its own currency, the Guinean franc, breaking from the French monetary zone.
Guinea aligns with the Soviet bloc and pursues a socialist, one-party state under Sekou Toure.
A Portuguese-backed amphibious invasion aimed at toppling Toure is repelled, deepening the regime's repression and paranoia.
Market women's protests against economic restrictions force Toure to liberalize some private trade policies.
Sekou Toure dies, and a military coup led by Lansana Conte ends his long authoritarian rule and the socialist experiment.
Conte survives an attempted coup and consolidates power, shifting Guinea toward a market economy.
A new constitution is approved by referendum, paving the way for a multiparty political system.
Lansana Conte wins Guinea's first multiparty presidential election amid opposition claims of fraud.
An army mutiny over pay escalates into an attempted coup, which Conte narrowly survives.
Cross-border fighting spills over from civil wars in Sierra Leone and Liberia, sending refugees into Guinea.
Mass strikes and protests against Conte's rule kill scores of people and force the appointment of a consensus prime minister.
President Lansana Conte dies, and a military junta led by Captain Moussa Dadis Camara seizes power in a coup.
Security forces massacre over 150 opposition demonstrators at a Conakry stadium, and Camara is shot and wounded by an aide.
Alpha Conde wins Guinea's first democratic presidential election, ending decades of authoritarian and military rule.
Long-delayed legislative elections are held, completing Guinea's transition to civilian democratic institutions.
The West African Ebola epidemic begins in southeastern Guinea, killing thousands and devastating the economy.
Alpha Conde is re-elected to a second term as president amid opposition allegations of irregularities.
The World Health Organization declares the Ebola outbreak in Guinea over after more than 2,500 deaths.
Conde wins a contested third term after a constitutional referendum that opponents say circumvented term limits, sparking deadly protests.
Special forces colonel Mamady Doumbouya overthrows President Alpha Conde in a military coup, suspending the constitution.
The ruling junta proposes a transition timeline to civilian rule amid pressure from the regional bloc ECOWAS.
Guinea's transitional government advances plans for a new constitution and eventual return to elected civilian government.
